# Grant Application Narrative

*We appreciate your interest in submitting a proposal to the Azim Premji Philanthropic Initiatives Pvt. Ltd and we thank you for working with us throughout the proposal process. Your designated APPI contact will continue to work collaboratively with you as you prepare your proposal to help you understand the connection between the APPI's relevant program strategy and the proposed project, as well as to respond to any questions you might have over the course of this process.*

*You are encouraged to communicate with your program manager to make sure that your efforts are aligned with the proposal requirements and that you are not expanding unnecessary time or energy in this process.*

*Answer all of the questions in this Proposal Narrative template and submit it for review and collaborative discussion.*

-   *Due to reporting requirements, all proposals must be submitted in English.*
-   *The proposal must be submitted in Word, as PDFs will not be accepted.*
-   *Please ensure word limit is followed where relevant*
-   *This is a proposal shaping document and not a commitment by APPI to fund the work.*

# Section 2: Overview of the Organization

**2.1 Describe your organization’s history, purpose and work, including your organisation's vision/mission** (maximum 150 words)**:**

Developmental Association for Human Advancement (DEHAT) was first established at the behest of some young people from a most backward district- Bahraich in Uttar Pradesh.

DEHAT first started out as a school for ‘Tharu’ tribals and forest children. Ten years later, on 21st of August 2000 it was registered under Society Registration Act 1860.

We firmly believe that no society can hope for sustainable development unless its children are assured of their survival, development, protection and participation in the decision making and development process.

**Vision:** A child-centred society in which all children enjoy the fullest scope of their rights.

**Mission:** To educate, enable & empower the deprived and underprivileged sections of society, in order to ensure the basic rights of children.

**2.2 List the major thematic area of the organisation’s work, approach:** *(service-based, rights-based, community-based, policy advocacy, other – please mention)*

DEHAT’s major thematic areas of work are well linked to the child rights to survival, development, protection and participation.

The core areas of interventions are as under:

1.  **Right to survival:**

Maternal and child health, Community Health, Malnutrition, COVID-19, Water and Sanitation related activities.

1.  **Right to Development:**

Livelihood (especially Agro-based livelihoods), Community Organizations, Education, community development and related activities.

1.  **Right to Protection:**

Human trafficking, child labour, child marriages and child protection related activities and projects.

1.  **Right to Participation:**

Promoting child leadership through different activities like Child Parliaments, Adolescent Girls Group, Youth Group etc.

***Thematic Focus***

***Beneficiary profile***

***Demography Covered***

***How long have you been working on this theme***

**2.3 – A Describe the key problems that you are trying to address as an organization. What does the organization do, who are your primary and secondary stakeholders,** (maximum 300 words):

**Key Problems and DEHAT efforts for solutions:**

1.  **Poverty:**

DEHAT feels that the root causes of poverty are several but predominantly, they are low literacy level, lack of people’s participation in local self-governance, lack of knowledge about Govt. schemes, lack of institutional sources of livelihoods etc. Agriculture is the main source of income but the major share of land holdings is very small (70% are below one acre). Along with this, the farmers are doing agriculture work in conventional methods and this increases input costs. This leads to great losses in agriculture income and migration. To address the issue, DEHAT has focussed on enhancing household agriculture income with innovative ways of farming, linking them with Govt. schemes, trainings and exposures, model demonstrations etc.

1.  **Human Trafficking:**

Majority of DEHAT’s intervention districts are located at Indo-Nepal border. Due to extreme poverty, severe floods and lack of employment sources, the migration from this area is very high. Very often, the children, youths and women are trafficked in the name of migration. Secondly, due to a porous border, these areas are also the main transit points of cross-nation trafficking.

DEHAT has been addressing this issue for a long time – through 18 hours interception booth at border, community education, sensitization and training of law enforcement agencies, formation of vigilance groups, rescue operations and identification of vulnerable families and linking them with Govt. schemes etc.

1.  **Violation of Child rights:**

Violation of child rights is one of the most common problem in the area. To address this issue, DEHAT works on child rights as a cross-cutting issue integrated with various projects.

To ensure protection of Child Rights, DEHAT operates 24X7 Child Helpline Service- 1098 in Bahraich and Shrawasti districts of UP. Along with this, DEHAT is well focused on children’s issues in all its project interventions.

1.  **Gender based discrimination:**

As is evident in other parts of country, the intervention areas of DEHAT too are affected with various deep-rooted gender-based discriminations.

To address the issue, DEHAT has taken this issue as cross-cutting issue for all the projects. We focus on gender sensitivity in all our CBOs through regular meetings and periodic campaigns.

1.  **Resistance about Govt. Health Services:**

In many villages or urbans, people resist to take the Govt. health services. The reasons are different. Some of the reasons are the developmental issues, non-availability of Govt. schemes, any previous experience of injustice, some health-related reasons etc.

DEHAT has taken these LODOR (Left out, Drop-out and resistant) villages and urban pockets

And is working intensively to save the lives of mothers and children through building local leaderships, resolving the myths or issues, community mobilization, sensitization of Govt. officials etc.

**2.3 – B How and why do you work with them, key strategic interventions and your delivery model?** (maximum 300 words):

**DEHAT’s approach of work:**

**1\. Behaviour change:** a. DEHAT believes that small changes in behaviour and attitude can change the entire life. Keeping this in view, DEHAT first focuses on the change in harmful community behaviours and practices.   
b. Behaviour change increases demand generation and access to rights and entitlements.

**2\. Demand Generation:** a. DEHAT feels that Govt. has various programs, schemes and policies for the benefit of communities and budget allocations are also enough in maximum sectors but the demand generation from the communities is always weak. To end any problem, the realization of the problem and demand generation for effective solution plays the first and basic role.   
Keeping this in view, DEHAT focuses on promoting community leadership and courage to raise their voices, sensitizes people and capacitates them to raise their demand before the duty bearers.

**3\. System strengthening:** DEHAT always tries its best to boost the existing efforts by the Govt. systems for solutions. It is because if the community behaviour is changed and community is demanding, then obviously the community will own any Govt. initiatives. So, by involving the communities and creating a positive environment, we always promote system strengthening. This process also boosts the motivation of Govt. authorities to work more effectively.

1.  **Providing services:** 

In situations where the community realizes the need and makes demands from Government but there are no ![](data:image/png;base64,)immediate options or the solution seems to be time-taking, then we provide alternates until the realization of Govt. service/scheme/solution. For example, if there is no school and children are out of school, then we first demand a school for the children and till its realization, we will start an alternative education centre in that village.  

**Some other operational details are as under:**

1\. DEHAT prefers to work in the hard-to-reach or underserved areas where the community lives in difficult circumstances.   
2\. Right from the beginning DEHAT ensures the community ownership at each level in all grassroots level interventions.   
3\. DEHAT promotes CBOs (Community Based Organizations) in all its intervention areas.   
4\. DEHAT promotes local leaders and cadres by appointing local youths as workers and engaging locals as volunteers.   
5\. DEHAT always promotes community-led initiatives on the issues.   
6\. DEHAT establish its field offices in the mid of its intervention/project areas and all the interventions are implemented through these offices. This office reports the progress to DEHAT headquarters on daily basis.   
7\. In each office of DEHAT, DTS (Daily Task Sitting) is conducted every day and the minutes are circulated to the DEHAT headquarters daily through WhatsApp. In this meeting, the staff shares the progress against the plan of the previous day and plan for the day.   
8\. Daily reporting from the field is mandatory on DEHAT's WhatsApp group.   
9\. DEHAT has its Core Committee in which each project leader are the members and the meetings are held on the 4th day of each month.

***Describe the Issues you work on, in the last 5 years***

***Key Strategies and Methodologies you follow to address it***

***Key achievements and challenges on how you addressed the problem***

***Right to Survival***

1.  *Maternal and Child Health*
2.  *Malnutrition*
3.  *Water and sanitation*

1.  *Community organization, Sensitization and Mobilization*
2.  *Raising demand for government provisions/schemes by the communities*
3.  *Increasing community ownership over Govt. programs*
4.  *Stakeholders engagement*
5.  *Maximum leverage with Govt. programs*

***Results:***

1.  *Converted 183 villages till date from resistant to recipient*
2.  *Activated 86 ICDS Centres.*
3.  *Regularized VHSNDs in 92 villages.*
4.  *Facilitated the referral of 654 SAM children to Nutrition Rehabilitation Centres.*
5.  *Recovered 546 SAM children through home-based rehabilitation methods.*
6.  *Made 13 villages (One in Bahraich and 12 in Amethi districts of UP) open defecation free.*

***Challenges:***

1.  *Seeking cooperation of Govt. officials.*
2.  *Lack of community sensitivity towards the problems.*
3.  *Local power politics*
4.  *Negative stakeholders*

***Solutions:***

1.  *Transparency*
2.  *Regular involvement of Govt. officials*
3.  *Regular engagement of communities.*
4.  *Trust and bonding relationships with community.*
5.  *Capacity building of organization team.*
6.  *Strengthening local leaderships*

***Right to development***

1.  *Agro-based livelihoods*
2.  *MNREGA Promotion*
3.  *Corruption free reach of Govt. Schemes*
4.  *Education*

1.  *Community organization, Sensitization and Mobilization*
2.  *Raising demand for government provisions/schemes by the communities Increasing community ownership over Govt. programs*
3.  *Stakeholders engagement*
4.  *Maximum leverage with Govt. programs*
5.  *Introducing new methods of agriculture, LEISA techniques and organic farming*
6.  *Maximum training, exposure and demonstrations.*

***Results:***

1.  *Enhancement in agriculture income from 7000 rupees/2.5-3 Bigha to atleast 35000 rupees/2.5-3 bigha.*
2.  *Sustainable models like multilayer farming, line sowing, mixed cropping, multi-cropping.*
3.  *50% reduction in ground water consumption for irrigation.*
4.  *Reduced migration by 50%.*
5.  *No child labour, child marriages and child trafficking from intervened families.*
6.  *Made 142 Schools model under RTE Act in Bahraich, Shrawasti and Amethi districts of UP.*
7.  *Mainstreamed 3968 out of school and child laborers (72% girls and 28% Boys) in Govt. Schools through our 60 alternative learning centres.*

***Challenges:***

1.  *Seeking cooperation of Govt. officials.*
2.  *Lack of community sensitivity towards the problems.*
3.  *Local power politics*
4.  *Negative stakeholders*

***Solutions:***

1.  *Transparency*
2.  *Regular involvement of Govt. officials*
3.  *Regular engagement of communities.*
4.  *Trust and bonding relationships with community.*
5.  *Capacity building of organization team.*
6.  *Strengthening local leaderships*

***Right to Protection***

1.  *Protection of children from any kind of abuse*
2.  *Child and Human Trafficking*
3.  *Missing children etc.*

1.  *Operation of 24x7 Helpline for children*
2.  *Training of Police Personals and other law enforcement agencies.*
3.  *Community organization, Sensitization and Mobilization*
4.  *Raising demand*
5.  *Increasing community ownership over Govt. programs*
6.  *Stakeholders engagement*
7.  *Maximum leverage with Govt. programs*

**Results:**

1.  *Rescue of 648 children, 362 women and 128 youths from the traffickers. Registered 52 FIRs and imprisonment to 56 traffickers (30th September-2020).*
2.  *Rescue, shelter and restoration of 1998 missing, bonded and trafficked children (September-2020)*
3.  *Removed and mainstreamed 683 child labours.*
4.  *Made 9 villages free from child marriages.*
5.  *Supported 10912 Children in last 10 Years in realizing their rights through helpline.*

***Challenges:***

1.  *Seeking cooperation of Govt. officials.*
2.  *Lack of community sensitivity towards the problems.*
3.  *Local power politics*
4.  *Negative stakeholders*

***Solutions:***

1.  *Transparency*
2.  *Regular involvement of Govt. officials*
3.  *Regular engagement of communities.*
4.  *Trust and bonding relationships with community.*
5.  *Capacity building of organization team.*
6.  *Strengthening local leaderships*

***Right to Participation***

*Promoting child leadership for child rights through different groups.*

1.  Formation of Child Parliaments, Adolescent Girls Groups, Anti-trafficking Action group etc.
2.  Training and exposure of Child leaders on their rights, advocacy methods, digital advocacy, system and structure etc.
3.  Facilitate the Advocacy and boost children voice for their rights.

**Results:**

1.  **1**82 Child Parliaments till date.
2.  Made 29 villages model through child leadership
3.  An environment to believe in child leadership.

***Challenges:***

1.  *Seeking cooperation of Govt. officials.*
2.  *Lack of community sensitivity towards the problems.*
3.  *Local power politics*
4.  *Negative stakeholders*

***Solutions:***

2.  *Regular involvement of Govt. officials*
3.  *Regular engagement of communities.*
4.  *Trust and bonding relationships with community.*
5.  *Capacity building of organization steam.*
6.  *Strengthening local leaderships*

**2.4 Describe the organisation (include information on leadership, governance, organisational structure and staff) and any anticipated changes in the organogram** *(In maximum 500 words describe your organogram, different level, roles and responsibilities, organizational structure, team size and capacity)*

**History of emergence:**

Developmental Association for Human Advancement (DEHAT) was first established at the behest of some young people from Bahraich district in Uttar Pradesh. Bahraich has been identified amongst 100 most backward districts of India by the Planning Commission.

DEHAT first started out as a school for ‘Tharu’ tribals and forest children. Ten years later, on 21st of Aug’2000 it was registered under Society Registration Act 1860.

We firmly believe that no society can hope for sustainable development unless its children are assured of their survival, development, protection and participation in the development process.

In 1990, the United Nations Convention on the Rights of the Child (CRC) came into effect establishing a comprehensive set of rights for all children. We realized that this cannot be translated into reality in absence of focused intervention on capacity development linking child rights policy and practice.

Thus, Children remain the nucleus of all our work through which we aim at human advancement. A child is affected first and foremost by his immediate family and neighbourhood situation. With this view, today DEHAT is involved in a multitude of community organization and development activities besides advocacy, awareness and Rights struggle. Our intervention at policy making and/or influencing government is driven in the interests of the children particularly those coming from marginalized and oppressed sections of the society.

**2.5 Has the organization developed a long term strategic plan (organization development, resource management and programmatic)**

**2.5 Describe your financial situation. List your existing funders and what services or interventions they support? (Data for the last five years)**

***Funding Area/ Type***

***Duration of funding***

***Details of the Supported Programme***

***Resources***

Childline India Foundation

Child Rights

2010 Onwards

Operation of 24X7 toll free helpline for children and strengthening child protection mechanism in **Bahraich and Shrawasti districts of UP.**

Financial Support

Caritas India

Human Trafficking

2016 Onwards

1.  Intervention in **Nawabganj and Mihinpurwa blocks** of Bahraich district (UP) bordering Indo-Nepal border of Bahraich and Jamunaha block of Shrawasti bordering Nepal.
2.  Formation and strengthening of community groups like Village Level Child Protection Committees, Women SHGs, Youth Groups etc.
3.  Formation and Strengthening of Child Parliaments, Anti-trafficking Action Group-AAG etc.
4.  Rescue, rehabilitation, repatriation and restoration of the victims of child trafficking, child marriages, human trafficking, child laborers from India and Nepal.

Financial and capacity building support

ACC Cements-CSR

Integrated Development

2012 Onwards

1.  Community Health
2.  Maternal and Child Health
3.  Education
4.  Women Literacy
5.  Computer Education
6.  Livelihood
7.  Water and Sanitation,
8.  Social Cohesion etc. in **Gauriganj block of Amethi district-UP.**

Financial Support

Indo-Global Social Service Society- IGSSS

Malnutrition

2018 Onwards

An initiative to address hunger and malnutrition from 2600 affected families of scheduled tribe, scheduled caste, traditional forest dwelling communities, OBCs and minorities etc. of **Mihinpurwa block of Bahraich district in UP, INDIA.**

This project includes, CBO formation, referral of malnourish children to Nutrition Rehabilitation Centres, plantation of fruit plants, WASH, strengthening ICDS program, enhancing livelihoods for food and nutrition security etc.

Financial and capacity building support

Tata Trusts

Agro-based livelihoods and Natural Resource Management

2012 to 2018

Project Area: **Mihinpurwa Block of Bahraich-UP**

1.  Identified 1600 most needy, vulnerable, and small land holding farmer households.
2.  Organized all families into 62 groups (16/17 each) and empowered them through multiple training sessions and exposures.
3.  Developed 36 farmers as Community Resource Persons (CRP) and provided them pieces of training, exposers with an objective to develop them as a village level trainer.
4.  Plantation of 38000 fruit plants.
5.  Activities to decrease input costs, increase production, facilitate marketing linkages, and to increase community access for the maximum mobilization of government schemes.
6.  Introduced the organic (home-made) manure and pesticide to increase the fertility of the soil.
7.  Leveraged with the government schemes i.e. NABARD, MNREGA, Labor, Horticulture, Agriculture department.

Financial and capacity building support

**2.6 What have been your major organizational achievements (***describe awards, recognitions to your organization and employees) – last 5 years*

**Manjunath Shanmugam Integrity Award-2009:  
**The **‘Manjunath Shanmugam National Integrity Award-2009’** was given to ‘DEHAT’ for its integrity, commitment and efforts against corruption, by the Manjunath Shanmugam Trust IIM-Bangalore. The MST is an International IIM alumnus’s initiative in memory of Manjunath Shanmugam-an alumnus of IIM-Lucknow and an Indian Oil Corporation officer, who was murdered while working against oil adulteration.

**India NGO Award-2010:  
**DEHAT is one among the four non-govt. organizations in the country, who are awarded with **India NGO Award-2010 (NGO of the Year)**. The Resource Alliance in association with the Rockefeller Foundation gives this prestigious award. India NGO Awards are the most coveted awards to honour NGOs adopting good standards and practices during implementation of their programmes. The organizations were shortlisted on the basis of highest standards of resource mobilization, accountability and transparency.

**Global NGO Award -2011:**

DEHAT was one among the five voluntary organizations (Water Aid UK, Ashi Naga-Japan, Head Strong- Ireland, Living Earth-Uganda and DEHAT from India) shortlisted at International level for Global Awards-2011. These organizations were shortlisted from the National winners of NGO Awards given by The Resource Alliance-UK.

**Dasra Girls Power Award-2014:**

DEHAT was honoured as finalist for “Education” category and the citation was given by Ms. Kavita Ramdas-The Asia Chief of Ford Foundation among 6 leading organizations from India. The Dasra Girls Power Awards recognizes impactful and innovative programs that work with adolescent girls in three distinct areas of interventions –**Health**, **Education** and **Life Skills**.

**Featured Social Enterprise of the Year-2015:**

DEHAT have been honored by the **Chief Minister of UP-Mr. Akhilesh Yadav** as Social Enterprise of the Year 2015 at ***‘Manch’*** Summit 2015 for its outstanding replicable model of Crop Intensification Systems among the farmers having less than average land holdings*.* ***‘Manch’*** is a Social Entrepreneurship summit to identify impactful social enterprises from across the country. This annual event is organized by Fifth Estate with the support of the Government of UP.

**World CSR Congress’s ‘Certificate of Merit’-2016:**

On 18th February 2016, DEHAT has been conferred with the **‘Certificate of Merit’** in the category of **Social Cause and Empowerment** at **‘India’s Most Ethical Companies Conference and Awards’.** The Award have been instituted by the 5th Edition of World CSR Congress, and recognize impact on the way business is conducted in a socially responsible way by fostering a culture of ethics and culture. A total number of 123 countries from across the Globe has participated at the Congress.

**NGO of the Year 2017:**

On 17th May 2017, DEHAT has been conferred with **‘The NGO of the Year 2017 Award’** under the Banner of **‘India International Corporate Social Responsibility (IICSR) Excellence Awards-2017’** at **PHD Chamber of Commerce, New Delhi, India.** In other categories of CSR, the awards were given to the JK Cement, Jindal Steels & Powers, Ultra-tech Cement, Max Hospital, Toyota-Kirlosker, Tech Mahindra Foundation and Dalmia Bharat Foundation etc.

**Menstrual Hygiene Management Award-2019**

This award was presented by Gramalaya- Hyderabad for the commendable work on menstrual hygiene in rural villages.

**Awards to the Founder and CEO**

**SONY’s Social Bravery Award-2012:**

Dr. Jitendra Chaturvedi-Founder and CEO has been awarded with gold medal of **‘Social Bravery Award**’ by the Bollywood Industry under the banner of ‘SONY Entertainment Network’ in Mumbai for his fight for The Rights of the Forest Community Dwellers who were surviving without any constitutional rights before **his interventions**.

**Pride of the Nation Award-2015:**

Dr. Jitendra Chaturvedi-Founder and Chief Executive has been awarded with ***“*Pride of the Nation Award-2015*”*** by The Former Governor of West Bengal, Jharkhand and Assam Mr. Syed Sibte Razi under the banner of “TIMES Group of Media Network” in Lucknow for his commendable efforts towards missing, trafficked, vulnerable or underprivileged children.

**Swayam Award-2016:**

Swayam Award-2016 was presented by the Chief Minister of Uttar Pradesh Mr. Akhilesh Yadav for his extra-ordinary contribution to change the life of 5000 traditional forest dwellers of Bahraich district of UP.

**Awards to the Communities We’re Working For**

**Swayam Awards for Empowering Women-2015:**

On 2nd Decemeber 2015**, Swayam Awards-2015** has been awarded to 12 adolescents from across Uttar Pradesh by ***Mr. Akhilesh Yadav*\- The Honorable Chief Minister of Uttar Pradesh with presence of Mahesh Bhatt (Director in Bollywood) & Anshu Gupta (Ramon Magsaysay Awardee).** ***Rekha***, ***Gulaicha*** (Adolescents) and ***Ms. Sunita Chakravarty*** (Woman Education Volunteer) got this award for their incredible & empowered contributions in their communities. Three of them are from DEHAT’s **‘*Swadhyaya*’**\-Rural Girl Child Education Programme, Child Rights Forum/Parliament and ***Mahila Adhikar Manch****.*

**#100Women Achievers Award (Joint effort of Facebook & MCWD, Govt. of India)-2016:**

On 22nd January 2016, #100WomenAchievers Award has been awarded to Mrs. Bhanumati by ***Dr. Pranab Mukharjee***\- **The Honorable President of India** and ***Menka Gandhi*\- Honourable Women & Child Development Minister.** She’s been awarded for her incredible leadership while DEHAT’s forest rights (***Azaadi)* Movement** (started in 2003) for 8 forest dwelling villages, which is now officially recognized as **Revenue Villages** and enjoying all The Government Schemes after 147 years of Neglect and Abuse.

Over 100 women have been selected from across the Country by asking Indians & NRIs from across the globe to vote each one of them through Facebook page of Ministry of Women and Child Development.

**Rani Lakshmi Bai Veerta Honor (UP State Gallantry Awards for women)-2016:**

DEHAT’s adolescent girl child leader Rekha (Prime Minister of DEHAT Child Rights Forum/Parliament) has been awarded with this very prestigious state level award given by Govt. of UP. This award has been given by Mr. Akhilesh Yadav-CM

***Third party evaluations (name of the evaluator)***

# Section 3: Program Implementation Details

**3\. 1 Programme Objective - strategies and key results (Please fill in the box)**

*(Describe the scope and approach programme, this should be a narrative of the principal results you would like to achieve)***:**

1\. Community organization and Institution building.

2\. Enhance productivity to ensure 12 months of food and nutrition security and increased agricultural income of at least 50% households from Rs 10000 per acre/per annum to Rs 60000 per acre/ annum

3\. A network of well-trained 80 Community Resource Persons.

4\. Enable citizen's/farmer's access to Rights, entitlements, government schemes and services

5\. Strengthen the organizational systems and programmatic expertise on Agro-based livelihoods domain  

***Describe Programme Strategies***

***Key Results***

-   The target group focus would be on the small and marginal farmers. The project would emphasize on inclusion of single women farmers.
-   The key focus would be on enhancing the effectiveness and applicability of the agriculture development models.
-   The project would focus on developing context (agronomy, topography, problems, opportunities etc.) specific Packages of Agricultural Practices (PoPs). In developing these packages, the key considerations would be the optimum use of the critical agricultural resources (land/soil and water), productivity increase, cost reduction through lessening of cost-intensive inputs, etc.
-   The project will have specific focus on improvement of the soil health profile; and, in particular, the soil organic health profile. The overall strategy for this purpose would be to sensitizing the farmers to the causes and consequences of the declining soil health and also on the means/methods to restore the same. The key strategy for this would be to make the farmers realize the importance of the role of cow/buffalos’ dung in reviving and maintaining the organic profile of the soil. The techniques/technologies to be promoted for this purpose would include propagation of improved methods/systems of gobar (cow dung) gas; composting/ recycling organic waste; and, green manuring \[through *dhaincha* (Sysbania) and leguminous crops (*moong*, *lobiya*/cow pea) cultivation.
-   Still further, to encourage, and also to enable, the farmers to channel the cow/buffalo dung to the agricultural land (in the form of manure) instead of using the same as fuel for cooking, plantation of trees (mainly for fuel and also for fodder and food), especially along the bunds, would be promoted among them. The trees appropriate for this purpose include *gliricidia* and *acacia*. In areas where the average size of land parcels has become very small (owing to persistent fragmentation) and is not amenable for tree plantation on bunds (as the trees would cast shadow on crops), cultivation of shrub type plants (*sysbania*, pigeon pea, etc.) will be promoted on bunds.
-   In the process of identifying/selecting appropriate technologies for developing context specific PoPs , the repository of knowledge available at agriculture universities and research institutions would be made use of (in the previous phase of the agriculture development intervention, the efficacy of this strategy has been well proven). Good linkage/relationship (and, if possible, formal) with these institutions and the scientists and experts therein will be built/maintained.
-   The emphasis on the institutional development component would be in focus. The project intends to allocate more resources (human, material) on building and capacitating an appropriate institutional base among the target community. Wherever community institutions are already in existence (SHGs, farmers’ clubs, etc.), an attempt would be made to bring them into the project’s fold.
-   For highlighting the agriculture/NRM related critical issues and problems from the project area, advocacy efforts with the concerned agencies and departments would be carried out. Some of these issues include credit and insurance support/services to farmers, the timeliness and quality of critical agricultural inputs of seed and fertilizers, etc. efforts will also be made to build rapport/linkage with the NRLM programme of the government of India.
-   The emphasis will also be on impacting the farmers at household level. This will be done through strengthening Community Based Organisation at Gram Sabha, Gram Panchayat and Block Levels. The community will be mobilised, organised and collectivised to voice out their concerns at each advocacy platform and access the government schemes offered by the provisions of state. The increased income through livestock and agro based livelihood options will be tracked through income tracking booklets and factsheets whereas fact sheets will be keeping a thorough check on where the increased income has been allocated at the household level and if it is utilised to ensure the rights of the children and other members of the family. It is worth noticing that we will be providing farmers with the kitchen garden and rooftop farming at the household level which will in turn ensure the nutrition and food security.
-   For the coordination role, the strategy would be to provide intensive handholding support to the field staff at all the project locations. Furthermore, major attention would be on the perspective and skills building of the field personnel associated with the project. For this purpose, a series of structured training exercises would be organised for them.

-   Farmers are collectivised to negotiate and advocate their issues and rights in all levels of local self-governance systems.
-   Overall food and nutrition security throughout the year by adoption of Natural Resource Management and LEISA techniques
-   A strong voluntary cadre who will exist and perform at the community level.
-   Increased per household Agro- & livelihoods based income through access to eligible govt. leverage, agriculture, livelihoods, and welfare schemes.
-   A trained and experienced team with in-depth understanding of Agro-based livelihoods domain and allied practices

**3.2 Problem statement** *(describe the problem, why is it a problem, what are the specific elements you are trying to address with this grant, what is the change you want to achieve and how are they reduce/ mitigate to the problem):*

**The Livelihood conditions**

There lies a dark phase inside this incredibly beautiful place. The majority of the population has no source of their livelihoods; the basic facilities are untouched due to the remote location. The monitoring authority does not conduct enough visits.

**Lost institutional sources of livelihoods:**

In addition to agriculture, there were also 5 major institutional sources of livelihoods:

1.  Stone collection and loading from Gerua River (3000 people engaged in labour, working for 8 months per year)
2.  Stone crusher plants (500 people engaged as labourers for 10 months)
3.  Saryu Canal project (3000 people engaged for 6 to 8 months)
4.  Forest department’s marketing depot (200 people engaged in collection, loading and unloading of forest products for 6 months) .
5.  Govt. of India’s Central State Farm (13000 Acres), where more than 3000 laborers were engaged.

But, unfortunately, all above sources were closed due to the Forest and Wildlife Protection Acts and wildlife sanctuary between 1994-2010. Closings of these sources have created a severe crisis of livelihood. The majority of the population has only agriculture as their source of income. They were growing the crops by using the traditional methods of farming.

**Availability of wages**

The women belonging to Dalit and most backward communities generally work as agriculture labour. However, this daily wage work is not available regularly for the entire month. During the busiest crop season, they get wage opportunities for hardly 15 to 18 days in a month. During off-season of agricultural work, the number of days comes further down. Since they have very little land holding, they are unable to produce required food grains like rice and wheat for the entire year. Male members of the family also work as agriculture labour and in the off-agriculture season, they migrate to cities for temporary jobs. They are deprived of opportunities to start alternate means of livelihood at local level.

**Food Insecurity**

Mihinpurwa Block has got one of the highest number of malnutrition cases in the District. The reason being is nutrition insecurity due to the unavailability of nutritious food. Lack of knowledge, unpaid or less than the norm wages and little land holding majorly contributes to such crises of food and nutrition insecurity.

**Food Produced vs Consumed**

**Rabi Cultivation (Wheat)**:

Wheat produced in 1 year: 2.5 quintals (average) per Bigha

Wheat consumed in 1 year by family of 5 individuals: 8.4 quintals

(1 Acre = 5 Bigha)

**Khareef Cultivation (Rice):**

Rice produced in 1 year: 2.5 quintals (average) per Bigha

Rice consumed in 1 year by a family of 5 individuals: 3.6 quintals

(1 Acre = 5 Bigha)

**Zaid Cultivation:** \*currently practiced by only 4-5% of the farmer families in the proposed area

Total grains produced in 1 Bigha in a year – 5 quintals.

**Total grains consumed in a year (family of 5) – 12 quintals.**

**Thus, food security is available to a family of five through their own production for 5 out of 12 months.**

**Agriculture and agriculture-based wage**

Primarily, the survival of target families is dependent on the very small agriculture land and agriculture wage in crop harvesting seasons. About 75% of the families are marginal with average land holding of up to 0.5 acre. And about 25% are landless families, who only depend on agriculture-based wage work; and some of them take land on lease/sharing basis for cropping purposes. They hardly produce and earn grains for the 4 to 5 months from their land as well as agriculture-based wage work in crop harvesting seasons.

About 25% of the target group is of Turmeric and Chilli cultivators. But due to no marketing facility, they are helpless to sell the product on just 30-40% of the market rates.

Agricultural income **as of now** are as follows:

**Agricultural Income:**

**Rabi Cultivation (Wheat)**:  
Input Cost: 3,750 per Bigha  
Output: 4,250 per Bigha  
Profit of 4 months per Bigha: 500  
Profit of 4 months per acre: 1,500 (1 Bigha\*5= 1 Acre)

**Khareef Cultivation (Rice):**

Input Cost: 4,280 per Bigha  
Output: 6,000 per Bigha  
Profit of 4 months per Bigha: 1,720 per Bigha  
Profit of 4 months per acre: 8,600 (1 Bigha\*5= 1 Acre)  
**Zaid Cultivation: \*currently practiced by only 4-5% of the farmer families in the proposed area**  
Input Cost: 5,825 per Bigha

Output: 9,600 per Bigha

Profit of 4 months per Bigha: 3,775

Profit of 4 months per acre: (1 Bigha\*5= 1 Acre) = 18,875

**Low productivity of milking animals**

Traditionally livestock are an integral part of the agricultural economy in this region. They have poor breeds with very low productivity. Animal husbandry is another major source of income apart from agriculture; but due to the low milk productivity they get very less margin in relation to investment over animals.

The nearest “Artificial Insemination Centre” is located at a distance of 70 Km. (Nanpara).

On other hand, poor facilities and veterinary services affect the milk productivity. The breed of milch animals is also a factor that affects productivity. Although there is a good market of buffaloes in the same block (Parwani Gaurhi Village-50 KM.), due to lack of investment capacity, the target families are unable to purchase high milk producing breeds of buffaloes. The scenario is that the target families are neither to purchase good buffaloes nor able to mobilize good veterinary services for animal care and small dairy unit management.

**Lack of marketing and linkages for milk and vegetables**

There are numbers of local markets in the area and the district head quarter is also at the distance of 105-120 Km; but the milk and vegetable producers are not getting the appropriate price of their products. The marketing process is unorganized. There is a lack of value chain in the marketing system so that they can get the better price for their product. On other hand, milk and vegetables producers need to produce milk and vegetables in bulk quantity to establish forward marketing linkage with milk collection centres of big dairy and Sabzi Mandi (organized vegetables market). The other problem is that the marginal vegetables producers are also unable to negotiate on prices of vegetables with Mandi people. In such a situation, there is a need to enable vegetables producers to negotiate with Mandi people through establishing a proper producers-led marketing chain.

**Poor access to credit facilities and unfair interest on local debt**

On one side, the SHGs do not have enough potential to fulfil their credit requirement for starting the micro enterprises. And on other hand, the local banks create problems in grading and CCL. Sometimes formalities and procedures of the banks become more complicated and time taking for SHG members. Generally, the banks do not follow the NABARD guidelines of SHG bank linkage. Therefore, there is need for intervention to institutionalize the bank linkage systems in banks as per the NABARD guidelines. Moreover, SHGs need to build their capacity accordingly to advocate their bank linkage issues with bankers.

The local money lenders give the loans not on interest but on crop. They give the loan on their own terms to repay the loan in form of grain at the rate decided only by him and the rates decided by the money lenders are almost half of the market rates.

There is a lack of other credit facilities like MFI to provide loan funds to SHG members. In such a situation, only they must depend on only banks for starting enterprises. And in case of emergency, they must go to moneylenders/landlords for taking loan on unfair interest by mortgaging their utensils, ornaments and land etc.  

**Poor irrigation facilities**

The origin of Saryu and Sharda canals is situated in the area but not a single farmer can take the benefits of these canals because of no minors. The whole irrigation depends on private diesel pumping sets and this facility costs Rs. 150/- per hour. Poor farmers, who have no irrigation facility, depend on rain, water logging and climatic conditions.

**No reach of Govt. Agriculture and Horticulture Schemes**

Due to the highly remote area, the farmers were totally out of reach of the Govt. Agriculture and Horticulture schemes. No extension programs run by the Govt. on agriculture-based livelihoods.

**3.3 Project History** - 5 years (*how the planned project relates to earlier, ongoing or other projects. If yes, what has been the programme duration and describe its operation model. Has a baseline study been done? Is it deepening, replication, scaling up, or a new intervention) Please include numbers of target groups/households (direct and in-direct), already covered:*

**DEHAT** has been working on the Agro-based livelihoods theme since 2012. However, prior to 2012, we were rigorously focusing and intervening to get the community’s basic rights including land and voting rights for 991 families through our community based Forest Rights Movement for more than 12 years. Till date DEHAT has impacted over 1600 farmer families (600 in FASAL Project and 1000 in Sujalam Sufalam Projects) of Mihinpurwa block directly with Agro-based livelihood interventions including Natural Resource Management.

**Nature and strategy of program**

To address the livelihoods issues like low productivity due to the attack of wild animals, water logging, lack of market facilities, high debt-ness, no access to bank loans, the nexus of moneylenders cum local grain merchants, lack of knowledge towards scientific and improved agricultural practices etc., DEHAT has worked in multiple phases:

1.  **FASAL Project: Supported by TATA Trust**

**Duration:** 3 Years (2012-2015)

**Target families covered:** 600

**About the Initiative:**

-   Identified 1000 most needy, vulnerable and small land holding farmer households
-   Organized all families into 62 groups (16/17 each) and empowered them through multiple training sessions and exposures
-   Developed 36 farmers as Community Resource Persons (CRP) and provided them multiple trainings, exposures with an objective to develop them as a village level trainer.
-   Plantation of 38000 fruit plants.
-   Activities to decrease input costs, increase production, facilitate marketing linkages, and to increase community access for the maximum mobilization of government schemes.
-   Introduced the organic (home-made) manure and pesticide to increase the fertility of the soil.
-   Promoted border plantation and vegetable cultivation to increase their livelihood.
-   Mobilized Resources from the government schemes i.e. NABARD, MNREGA, Labor, Horticulture, Agriculture department.
-   Worked on Integrated Natural Resource Management (INRM) comprehensively in collaboration with Horticulture and Agriculture Departments  
    

1.  **Sujlam Suflam Project: Supported by TATA Trust**

**Duration:** 3 Years (2015-2018)

**Target Families covered:** 1000

**About the Initiative**

Same as FASAL

1.  **Sustainability Phase of above 2 projects: Supported by DEHAT itself.**

**Duration:** 2018 Onwards

**Target Families covered:**

**About the Initiative:  
Same as FASAL.**

1.  **Empowering Rural Women (ERW) Projects: Supported by TATA Trust**

**Duration:** 2010 Onwards (Funded from 2011-12 and self sustained until now)

**Target Families covered:**

**About the Initiative:**

Organize and empower 1500 women of 10 Gram Panchayats to ensure

1.  Right to Food
2.  Right to Work (100% leverage from MNREGA)

Their participation in the local self-governance system.

1.  **SuPoshan Project- Supported by (IGSSS) and MISEREOR-Germany**

**Duration:** 2019 Onwards

**Target Families covered:**

**About the Initiative:**

An initiative to address hunger and malnutrition from 2600 affected families of scheduled tribe, scheduled caste, traditional forest dwelling communities, OBCs and minorities etc. of Bahraich district, Uttar Pradesh.

This project includes:

1.  CBO formation,
2.  Referral of malnourished children to Nutrition Rehabilitation Centres,
3.  Plantation of fruit plants, WASH, strengthening ICDS program, concerning the Integrated Natural Resource Management, and nutrition security etc.**  
      
    Operating Model:**
4.  Identified 1600 (In total) most needy, vulnerable and small land holding farmer households, organized them into 62 groups (16/17 each) and empowered them through multiple training sessions and exposures.
5.  Facilitated the groups and selected two to five farmers from each group and provided them with the full-fledged support to create a demonstration of new techniques and rest were motivated to adopt the techniques and innovations at their own cost at very low rates.
6.  Developed 36 farmers as Community Resource Persons (CRP) and provided them pieces of training, exposers with an objective to develop them as a village level trainer. These CRPs were developed to train the villagers on the best farming practices to making the process sustainable. Now a network of 36 farmers have been established by our efforts and they are continuing the facilitation process. These CRPs also provides monitoring and handholding support and act as a bridge between us and the community. They are also accountable and responsible for the village level activity. Our supervisors have direct touch with the CRPs.
7.  Activities to decrease input costs, increase production, facilitate marketing linkages, and to increase community access for the maximum mobilization of government schemes.
8.  Implemented multi-layer farming, mixed cropping, line sowing, high value crops etc.
9.  Introduced the organic (home-made) manure and pesticide to increase the fertility of the soil.
10.  Promote border plantation and vegetable cultivation to increase their livelihood.
11.  Leveraged resources from the government schemes i.e. NABARD, MNREGA, Labor, Horticulture, Agriculture department.
12.  Established Farmer’s Resources Center across the villages for the orientation and facilitation for the farmers and community.
13.  Technical Resource Person visits by every month cycle with an objective to facilitate the farmers on the problems related to the crop or vegetable cultivation.
14.  Awareness consultation meetings with the community for making them aware about the government schemes with an objective to make their access towards the schemes.

**Core Strategies:**

1.  Change in traditional practices and farmer’s behaviour increasing drudgery, input costs and losses.
2.  Develop local agriculture experts within the farmer community.
3.  Inter-sectoral coordination
4.  Maximum leverage of Govt. agricultural and horticultural schemes directly to the farmers.
5.  Connect with financial networks.

**The linkage with proposed project:**

The proposed project is well linked to the previously implemented projects and replication with some added innovations.

**3.4 What is the operating model** **or strategy?** (*visual representation on how organization delivers values to its beneficiaries and program delivery structure – programme staff roles and responsibilities )*

**Operating Model:**

1.  **Community Organization and Institution building:**

DEHAT proposes to organize the target communities in small groups of 15-20 families each, cluster, and block level federations. These institutions will help to enhance the project impacts against low-cost inputs.

These institutions will be the strong pillars for the sustainability which will work through the following tiers:

**Direct Beneficiary to be impacted:**

-   1.  Project beneficiary families – 1500 (Approximately 82% saturation in every village)
    2.  No. of villages – 20 in Mihinpurwa block (average 75 families in 1 village)
    3.  No. of AAS groups: 80

**Breakdown:** On an average 4 Aajeevika Adhikaar Sangathan (AAS) /village \* 20 village = 80 AAS groups  
(AAS group: 15 – 20 members in 1 AAS group. There will be 3 to 4 AAS groups in 1 village according to no. of families in the village)

-   1.  No. of Cluster federations: 5

**Breakdown:** 1 cluster per 4 villages \* 20 villages = 5 Clusters.  
Therefore, 1 cluster per 16 AAS groups (each cluster will have 30-50 members (2-3 leaders from all the AAS groups) of the 5 villages)

-   1.  No. of Block level federations:1

**Breakdown:** 40 members (Leaders or members from all clusters) will be part of the block level federation.

**S. No**

**Group**

**Roles and Responsibilities**

1.

AAS group

-   Regular tracking of each and every families at village level
-   Collective bargaining negotiation power through bottom to top approach with Gram Panchayats and the concerned Government Departments
-   Protection of participating target families from any kind of exploitation at the village level

2.

Cluster federation

-   Exchange of information from village level to cluster level on regular basis
-   Collective sharing and learning
-   Collective bargaining negotiation power through bottom to top approach with Gram Panchayats and the concerned Government Departments
-   Protection of participating target families from any kind of exploitation at the village level

3.

Block federation

-   Exchange of information from cluster to block level
-   Monitor and track performance of clusters
-   Representation of all community sections at block level
-   Act as key management stakeholders for the FRC.
-   Maintain periodic communication with block-level government officials
-   Do Market linkages through FPO at appropriate times in Block and District level for the program farmers.

**Importance & Impact:**

1.  **Capacity building of communities:**

Capacity building of community through training, sensitization, orientation, and exposures will be an important strategy for leadership development and building expertise at local level.

S. No

Training

Content/Curriculum of the training

1.

Capacity building of farmer Group Leaders on effective leadership and group management

-   Consensus Building
-   Realizing the sense of community
-   Leadership Qualities
-   CBOs and group sustainability
-   Grievance redressal and conflict management
-   Documentation of each workings of the group
-   Group Dynamics
-   Effective review and planning of each meetings

2.

Induction on Govt. schemes for agriculture, horticulture and allied sectors of livelihoods

-   Sector-wise induction of agriculture and other welfare schemes to the community
-   Eligibility Criteria for the schemes
-   Documentation to access the schemes

3.

Training of farmers on Gender based discrimination

-   Exchange of roles in the farming
-   Understanding and Reducing gender inequality
-   Recognizing woman as farmers and farmer leaders
-   Calculation of work without pay/unnoticed work when it comes to woman’s working especially in their own fields
-   Role of Shared responsibilities in Farming
-   Equal and rightful treatment to woman in the domestic and public spaces
-   What went right and not so right until now

4.

Training of farmers on the basic concept of “Low External Inputs Sustainable Agriculture-LIESA practices”

-   Introduction of and understanding LIESA concepts, practice and techniques
-   Importance of LIESA
-   Timeline of LIESA based Agriculture
-   Expected Impact of practicing and not practicing LIESA

5.

Training on mixed farming

-   Introduction of and understanding mixed farming concepts, practice and techniques
-   Importance of Mixed Farming
-   Vegetable timeline of mixed farming
-   Developing nurseries
-   Distancing of plantation
-   Expected Impact of practicing and not practicing Mixed Farming
-   Resources needed to get the maximum outcome and invest the minimum input

6.

Training on natural resource management and climate change

-   Introduction of and understanding the concepts of Natural Resource Management and Sustainable Agriculture
-   Pest Management
-   Soil Management
-   Water Management
-   Consequences of not using Natural Resource Management
-   How agriculture is contributing to adverse climate change?
-   Farmer’s contribution to mitigate the climate change

7.

Training on organic pest management

-   Recognising if and when the plants needs pest control
-   Making of organic Pesticide
-   Quantity Analysis of pesticide application

8.

Training of farmers on multi-layer farming techniques

-   Understanding multi-layer farming
-   Benefits of multi-layer farming techniques in comparison to traditional practices
-   Considering combinations while applying multi-layer farming
-   Dos and Donts for Multi-layer Farming
-   Resources needed for multilayer farming

9.

Training on organic manuring and composting

-   Introduction of organic manuring and composting
-   Process of making Organic Compost and manuring
-   Methods of making Organic Compost and manuring (HEAP and Vermi-compost)
-   Green Manuring through Dhaincha
-   Timeline of making and application
-   Benefits of organic manuring and composting
-   Consequences of not using organic manures

10.

Training on scientific turmeric and ginger cultivation

-   Introduction to scientific turmeric and ginger cultivation
-   Process of plantation and harvesting
-   Timeline of the inputs to be given to the crops
-   Benefits of scientific turmeric and ginger cultivation

11.

Cross-Learning exposure trips to ideal place of “Sustainable Agriculture and Low External Inputs Sustainable Agriculture-LIESA practices”

-   One to one dialogues with the farmers on the already practicing fields within the intervention areas
-   FAQ sessions with each other
-   Defining the idea of LIESA on practicing field

12.

Training of mobile user farmers on digital accessibility of schemes and leverage

-   Introduction to Government Schemes
-   Accessing Government provisions through 35 mobile applications, websites and sector-wise mobile helplines offered by the government
-   Documentation and tracking of progress
-   Review and Follow-up through online and offline modes

1.  **Input Support:**

DEHAT will promote Input Supports of innovative practices on agriculture and horticulture on the farms of willing farmers in all intervention villages.

**Sl. No.**

**Activity**

**Resources provided by project support**

**Resources contributed by community/Leverage**

1

Input Support on Multi Layer Farming with Nursery support

Seed, Net and expert support

Bamboo Pillers, labour, fertilizer, Ploughing, irrigation etc.

2

Input Supports on Inter/Mixed Cropping

Seed and expert support

Labour, fertilizer, Ploughing, irrigation etc.

3

Input Supports on Organic Manuring, and no-cost Input Support on Micro-nutrients and organic pest control

Pipes for HEAP composting and Expert support

Labour and space

4

Input Supports on Modern Vegetable Production Input Supports (Tomato on Trellis, Chilli, Trench farming for Mistletoe (Banda), Cabbage, Cauliflower, Garlic, Onion etc.), line sowing and distancing with nursery support

Seed, Seed-drill machine and expert support

Labour, fertilizer, Ploughing, irrigation, material for Trellis etc.

5

Input Supports on High value crops- Ginger and Turmeric

Seed and expert support

Labour, fertilizer, Ploughing, irrigation etc.

6

Input Supports on Roof top farming and Nutrition Garden

Seed kits

Labour, fertilizer, Ploughing, irrigation etc.

7

Input Supports on Fruit cultivation- Banana

Seedlings and expert support

Labour, fertilizer, Ploughing, irrigation etc.

**\*NOTE: Nursery –** The nurseries will be created for every Multi-layer Input Supports. 140 nurseries will be built for 140 multilayer Input Supports. The plot of land for nurseries will be selected through volunteering by willing women farmers and they will sell the nursery when ready. Preference will be given to farmers/single-women farmers who own the smallest land holdings. Besides being for sale in the market, these nurseries will be available as resources for project beneficiaries. This will further increase income generation for the smallest land-holding farmers. Vegetables like tomato, chilli, cabbage, cauliflower, onion, garlic, banda and lentils can be grown in the nursery.

In the first year, IEC will be developed in multiple forms (booklets, video tutorials, pamphlets) which will be used during trainings and Input Supports and distributed to the participants. Posters might be developed specifically for activities or used from existing content and copies will be printed according to activity requirements.

1.  **Adoption:**

DEHAT will motivate maximum farmers to adopt maximum innovative practices developed in Input Supports.

1.  **Leverage and Intersectoral Coordination:**

There are several Govt. schemes and resources but not reaching to the eligible farmers. DEHAT will facilitate all eligible farmers to get maximum benefits of these schemes.  
**Major schemes to be leveraged:**

**Health:** All the eligible provisions made under Ayushman Bharat (only through Ayushman Health Card), and Integrated Child Development Services (ICDS) schemes  
**Education:** All the eligible schemes under Right to Education, 2009.

**Village Infrastructure Development:** Leverage through Gram Panchayat Development Plan (GPDP) led 14th Finance Commission and 15th Finance Commission funding under PRI

**Child Development:** All the provisions made under Juvenile Justice Act, Child Sponsorship and Foster care

**Agriculture and Livelihood:** MNREGA, subsidies and schemes provisioned under Agriculture and Horticulture Department

**3.5 List the major activities and ensure that they align to the results in the Result Framework** *(mention the proposed number of unit interventions and target group with numbers proposed to be covered)*

***Major Activities/ Key Interventions***

***Key Outcomes***

**Community organization and Institution building**

1.  Formation of 80 small groups of 15-20 farmers as Aajeevika Adhikaar Sangathan- AAS.
2.  Formation of 5 cluster level federations; (1 cluster for every 4 villages or 16 AAS)
3.  Formation of 1 Block level federation
4.  Capacity building training of AAS leader farmers on effective leadership and group management (3 LeadersX80 AAS GroupsX4 (HY-1, HY-4, HY-6 & HY-8).
5.  Capacity building trainings on Gender based discrimination (3 LeadersX80 AAS GroupsX4 (HY-1, HY-4, HY-6 & HY-8)
6.  District level stakeholder interface meetings with all stakeholders
7.  Block level interface meetings with stakeholders
8.  Village level interface meetings with stakeholders

**Farmers are collectivised to negotiate and advocate their issues and rights in all levels of local self-governance systems.**

**Enhance productivity to ensure 12 months of food and nutrition security and increased agricultural income of at least 50% households from Rs 10000 per acre/per annum to Rs 60000 per acre/ annum**

**Inputs and Input Supports: \***Average 2 Input Supports will be done with the same individual (Rabi & Khareef), project will try to cover all 1500 target families with minimum 1 type of Input Support

1.  **Trainings of farmers on the basic concept of “Natural Resource Management based farming and LIESA"**

\***What constitutes LEISA as per DEHAT’s implementation process?**

LEISA will include following major components:

**Soil Health** - Includes green manuring, vermi-compost, HEAP technique etc.

**Nutrient management-** Include farm made organic nutrient-vermi-wash.

**Organic Pest Management –** Include the farm made pesticide of Garlic, Tobacco and Indica (Neem) leaves and a pesticide made with buttermilk, indica and copper coin etc.

**Groundwater conservation-** The following techniques will be implemented to ensure groundwater conservation techniques in agricultural practices

-   Mulching
-   Raised-bed farming
-   Trench Farming
-   Bunding

Line sowing and distancing between plants and rows

**Organic manuring** include dhaincha (for green manuring), vermi-compost, heap compost and other methods.

The above practices will be implemented for all Rabi, Khareef and Zaid crops**.**

1.  **Input Supports on Multi Layer Farming:** *Machan is a technique of multi-layer farming. Farmers can get two or more crops at a time in the same field and at the same duration. Furthermore, it decreases the input cost of farmers, increasing the quality and quantity of production. In this technique, the water-saving factor is most important; water used for irrigation is half of the quantity used in general techniques. Two or more crops are irrigated at the same time and with less quantity of water than required to irrigate one crop. Machan farming uses net roofing to grow a second crop above the ground-level crop. For example, farmers may grow turmeric or ginger along with bitter gourd and onion and radish with bottle gourd.*
2.  **Trainings on mixed farming**
3.  **Input Supports on Inter/Mixed Cropping:** *DEHAT proposes to promote intercropping through line sowing and maintain a distance between lines to promote inter-cropping like maize with okra and Banana with lentil, baby corn with sugarcane etc.*
4.  **Trainings on organic pest management, organic manuring and composting and micro nutrients**
5.  **Input Supports on Organic Manuring, Micro-nutrients and organic pest control:** *To reduce the dependency on chemical fertilizers with an objective to save and manage the natural resources, DEHAT proposes to introduce the organic manure-related practices in the area. DEHAT propose to promote farmers to prepare and use Vermi-wash as a micronutrient in their fields.  
    Farmers make vermin-wash with live earthworms, crushed stones, sand and water. Farmers use 1000 ml. with 10 litres of water as micro-nutrient for crops.  
    This liquid supplements all kind of deficiencies and also increases plants immunity. Similarly, DEHAT will also promote pesticide made with tobacco, Azadirachta indica and garlic.*
6.  **Input Supports on line sowing and distancing:** *We propose to promote line sowing in each crop. In this method, we maintain a certain distance from the plant to plant and row to row. This space provides the plants with proper space and enough nutrition to grow.*
7.  **Trainings on scientific turmeric and ginger cultivation**
8.  **Input Supports on High value crops- Ginger and Turmeric:** *High-value crop generally refers to non-shapeable agricultural crops such as vegetables, ornaments, and species. Under high-value crops, DEHAT propose to promote Banana, Ginger and Turmeric. These crops have good market potential.*
9.  **Input Supports on Rooftop farming and Nutrition Garden:** *Under this activity, DEHAT will benefit those farmers, who are landless or have very small landholding. Under this activity, DEHAT will provide good quality vegetable seeds to the families. The entire cultivation will be done on the roof.*
10.  **Input Supports on Fruit cultivation- Banana:** *DEHAT will promote banana and papaya cultivation in the intervened area.*
11.  **Cross learning exposure visits within the intervention villages**

**Increased food and nutrition security from 5 months to 12 months by adoption of Natural Resource Management and LEISA techniques**

**NOTE: Production of Rabi & Khareef:** Combined grain output (Rabi + Khareef) per bigha will increase from the current 5 quintals/year/bigha *(please check 3.2 food produced vs consumed for details)* to 7.5-8 quintal/year/bigha through Crop Intensification System and/or line sowing. In Crop Intensification System, the seeds are sown at a distance of 10-12 inches from one another in a straight line. These lines also have 10-12 inches gap between each other. This ensures that each seed receives the maximum needed nutrients and are able to produce 1.5 more times grain output.

**Production of Zaid:** When vegetable cultivation is done in the 3 months between Rabi and Khareef. Thus, by adopting the above practices, agricultural income increases from Rs. 10000/acre/annum to Rs. 60000/acre/annum.

-   **Total grains (Rabi+khareef) consumed by family of 5/year = 12 quintals Breakup: 1 quintal (70kg Wheat, 30 kg rice) grain is consumed in 1 month**
-   Currently Total grains (Rabi+khareef) produced /bigha/year = 5 quintals
-   With program support, Increased production (Rabi+khareef) /bigha/year = 7.5 – 8 quintals
-   With program support, Increased production (Zaid) vegetables/bigha/year = 3-4 quintals
-   **With program support, total agricultural output/year = 11-12 quintals**

Thus, food security increases from 5 months to 12 months.

**A network of well-trained 80 Community Resource Persons**

1.  Identification of 80 pro-active, progressive and leader Community Resource Persons as "Krishi Mitras"
2.  1 Residential Training on Rabi and 1 non-residential training on Khareef of Krishi Mitras on the basic concept of “Natural Resource Management based farming and LIESA"
3.  1 Two-Days training on Rabi and 1 non-residential training on Khareef of Krishi Mitras on Crop Protection
4.  1 Two-Days Training on Rabi and 1 non-residential training on Khareef of Krishi Mitras on organic methods of manuring, pest management and micro-nutrient
5.  Exposure visits of Krishi Mitras on sustainable agro-based models in UP and other States.
6.  Monthly meetings of Krishi Mitra for re-orientation, review and updates with Project Thematic expert and Project Coordinator.

After receiving the above trainings, the Krishi Mitras will support their peer farmers in implementing these techniques.

**Note:** The Community Resource Persons or Krishi Mitras will be experienced and trained farmers from the community who are enthusiastic and eager. Some of the CRPs will be farmers who have previously participated with DEHAT on Agro-based projects in the last 8 years, and has been connected to various schemes. They are currently practicing sustainable agriculture through the proposed techniques and have experienced high income generation which has secured the livelihood, education, nutrition of their households.

The Krishi Mitras will be selected through voluntary participation to act as advisors in agricultural techniques and practices and provide knowledge support to the new beneficiaries in their respective villages. Each Krishi Mitra will support 5 farmers for 1 year (Rabi, Khareef, Zaid) intensively. In 5 years, 1 Krishi Mitra will have supported 25 individual farmers, 80 Krishi Mitra will have supported 2000 individual farmers.

Their roles and responsibilities will be as follows:

1.  Frequent & periodic visit to the agricultural farm through hands-on participation.
2.  Guide individual farmers in Crop Protection
3.  Information & other knowledge sharing regarding best seeds, fertilizers, input resources.
4.  Sharing of experiences and solution in case of diseases or other complications.
5.  Guide in making organic manure, fertilizer and pesticides
6.  Guide in implementing Natural Resource Management and LEISA techniques

  

**A strong voluntary cadre who will exist and perform at the community level**

**Enable citizen's/farmer's access to Rights, entitlements, government schemes and services**

Farmers Resource Centre will be registered as CSC Jan Seva Kendra for that Gram Panchayat. The initial registration will be done in HY1 and then it will be renewed every year. The Block Level Federation will be in charge of the management and administration of the FRC (and will continue to be so after the project ends).

The Jan Seva Kendra will be the key access points for the following provisions and services:

1.  Social Welfare schemes
2.  Healthcare, financial, education services
3.  Agriculture and Horticulture schemes
4.  E-governance services (Centre will charge a fee as prescribed by Govt. of UP)

The centre will function for minimum 8 hours on all working days. On all Sundays and Public Holidays, the Centre will function for 4 hours. A candidate having the qualifications mentioned in the CSC Guidelines will be appointed to work at the Jan Seva Kendra and fulfil all the responsibilities as required.

1.  Facilitation of farmers through “Farmers Resource Centre” to avail the benefits of Govt. schemes
2.  Trainings on Govt. schemes for agriculture, horticulture and allied sectors of livelihoods
3.  Trainings of mobile-user farmers/Youths on digital accessibility of schemes and leverage
4.  Registration of farmers on Govt. portals

**Increased per household Agro- & livelihoods-based income through access to eligible govt. leverage, agriculture, livelihoods, and welfare schemes.**

**Strengthen the organizational systems and programmatic expertise on Agro-based livelihoods domain**

1.  **Institutionalisation of community cadre-**Inclusion of youth community Cadre within the organisation as DEHAT Fellows.
    -   1.  Develop policy and Standard Operating Procedure for DEHAT Fellowship Programme
        2.  **Organise-**Selection of potential leaders from existing youth cadre to become a part of the organisational structure as DEHAT Fellows
        3.  Induction, orientation, training and capacity building of DEHAT Fellows to intensify and spread DEHAT’s vision, mission and theory of change on ground as well as build their individual capacities
        4.  Continuation of three-year Fellowship Programme (5 individuals/year) on rotation basis every year
2.  **In-House Management and Operations**
3.  Revise existing organisational policies to make them more inclusive
4.  Programme Compliance Policies and Practices-Develop Policies, Standard Operating Procedures and formation of concerned committees with Azim Sherwani
5.  Team realignment with organisation’s vision and mission
6.  Gender and Sexual Minorities (GSM) sensitive policies, orientation and practices
7.  **Training of DEHAT-FASAL team**
8.  **Training and Capacity Building:**
9.  1 Training for Director and Programme Coordinator with chosen Resource person/institution
10.  1 Training for DEHAT’s Cluster Level Team
11.  1 Training Community Resource Person (CRP)
12.  **Exposures and visits with Gagan Sethi**

**A trained and experienced team with in-depth understanding of Agro-based livelihoods domain and allied practices**

**3.6 Risk Mitigation** *(What are the implementation risks and factors that may cause the programme to derailed or delayed)* **\- Table or Illustration of Risk/Mitigation Strategy (political, programmatic, financial risk, operational risks) check list and means of mitigation**

**Risk Category**

**Yes/NO**

**If Yes, Details and means of Mitigation**

*Political*

Yes

Some political influencers may demand benefits from the project.

**Mitigation:**

DEHAT will focus on strengthening community institutions to answer these political challenges. DEHAT will initiate the community-based decision making process.

*Programmatic*

Yes

1.  Farmers are not ready for implementing newly introduced package of practices.
2.  Financial constraints before farmers for inputs.

**Mitigation:**

DEHAT will motivate and sensitize farmers towards adoption of practices through trainings, exposures and cross learning visits to the practitioners.

For financial constraints, DEHAT will connect farmers and groups with the banking services in the beginning and then increased income will support .

*Financial*

No

*Operational*

Yes

1.  Staff turnover
2.  Tracking of progress

**Mitigation:**

DEHAT will maintain the staff motivation through encouraging working environment, respect, rewards and regular salaries.

To track the progress DEHAT will work more to strengthen its MIS systems and reporting.

**3.7 After the end of the funding period, what are the measures that will be taken to ensure continuation of the work? Please describe any reasons for what would not be possible**

-   From each village, DEHAT will identify 5 sharp and good performing farmers as Community

Resource Persons (CRPs). During the project duration, DEHAT will train

them to capacitate as experts. After the project, these CRPs will lead the processes further.

-   Changed agricultural practices will lead to sustainability.
-   The registration on the Govt. websites and linkages with Govt. schemes will lead to sustainability.

**3.8 What is the project implementation organogram/ model of the programme (Role and key responsibilities)? If names are already decided, kindly add them along with gender**

**Upload document various staff levels and salaries slabs**

*Programme Staff (for APPI Programme)*

Staff (Name)

Role and Responsibilities

Tenure

Education/Qualification

Experience

Project Director (Part Time)

1.  To guide the entire team properly.
2.  Coordination, collaboration and liasoning with state actors and Govt. departments
3.  Proper monitoring
4.  Ensure all the compliances
5.  Quality assurance

Part Time

DEHAT’s Chief Executive

MA (Development)

5 Years

Project Manager (Full Time)

1.  Proper planning, supervision and monitoring of project activities
2.  Coordination with all the stakeholders
3.  Preparation of reports and documentation
4.  Capacity building of staff
5.  Conduct all kind of meetings needful for the success of project targets.

Full Time

Masters in Development or Social Work or equivalent

3-5 Years

MIS cum Training Manager-1

1.  Developing data collection mechanism
2.  Capacity building and orientation of staff for data collection
3.  Develop and maintenance of Management Information System
4.  Provide data support to the team for proper advocacy and improvement etc.
5.  Prepare timely data reports and submit the same to Project Manager

Full Time

Masters in Development or Social Work or equivalent

1-3 Years

Cluster Coordinators-4

1.  Responsible for day-to-day project activities at farmers and village level.
2.  Community organization and mobilization.
3.  Maintain village level data and records
4.  Day to day onsite visits
5.  Filling Income Tracking Sheets of each family

Full Time

10th or above

3 Years at grassroot level development/community mobilization work

Accounts Officer

1.  Maintenance of finance and accounts
2.  Prepare and submit periodical accounts statements to the donor
3.  Support periodical audits

Part Time

B. Com. or M. Com. Or equivalent

With 3 years’ experience or M. Com. With 2 years’ experience

**3.9 Describe any government related partnership and future leverage that is expected to come out through this programme**

**The following linkages are proposed:**

1.  Farm Science Centre -Bahraich
2.  Crop Research Station, Bahraich (A unit of Acharya Narendra Dev University of Agriculture and Technology)
3.  Agriculture Department-Govt. Of UP
4.  Horticulture Department-Govt. Of UP
5.  Veterinary Department-Govt. of UP
6.  District Administration-Bahraich
